name: Suez Canal Blockage 2021
description: A geography-driven notebook of the Ever Given blockage showing the grounding point, queue build-up, salvage timeline, and the alternate route around the Cape.

sides.name: Incident core
sides.description: The grounding only became a global story because the ship, the canal authority, and the salvage effort each occupied different but interlocking roles in the response.

subUnits.name: Ever Given
subUnits.description:
The ship whose grounding transformed a transit corridor into a global chokepoint failure.
- Role: Physical blockage at the center of the story
- Geographic anchor: Wedged across the canal south of the Great Bitter Lake with little room for bypass traffic.
- Why it matters: The ship is the incident, but also the lens through which the fragility of the whole route became visible.
- What to watch: Position, salvage progress, hull condition, and the timing of refloating.

media.caption: Satellite view of Ever Given blocking the canal.

subUnits.name: Suez Canal Authority
subUnits.description:
The authority that managed traffic, coordinated the response, and then pursued the legal and financial aftermath.
- Role: Traffic control and response authority
- Geographic anchor: Managed the canal system from an Egyptian infrastructure and administrative network centered along the waterway.
- Why it matters: It linked on-scene response to global communication, convoy planning, and post-incident canal governance.
- What to watch: Salvage updates, convoy release sequencing, and institutional credibility after reopening.

subUnits.name: Salvage coalition
subUnits.description:
Tugs, dredgers, pilots, and technical advisers whose progress became the key operational clock for the whole crisis.
- Role: Refloating and channel clearance effort
- Geographic anchor: Concentrated at the grounding site and around the Great Bitter Lake where released traffic would later be sequenced.
- Why it matters: The salvage timeline drove every downstream decision by waiting ships and route planners.
- What to watch: Dredging pace, tug effectiveness, tide windows, and whether the hull could be safely shifted.

sides.name: Network effects
sides.description: The line between local incident and global disruption was crossed once waiting traffic and alternate routes had to be actively managed.

subUnits.name: Global liner networks
subUnits.description:
Container lines and cargo planners that had to convert canal uncertainty into concrete schedule, fuel, and inventory decisions.
- Role: Network adaptation and rerouting
- Geographic anchor: Spread across both queues and the alternate Cape route, with effects extending far beyond Egypt.
- Why it matters: This actor makes the incident global; without network delay, the grounding would remain a local engineering story.
- What to watch: Queue tolerance, diversion triggers, downstream port congestion, and vessel sequencing after reopening.

events.title: Ever Given grounds across the canal
events.description: A single grounding turned the canal into a complete blockage and instantly converted a local navigation incident into a global logistics shock.

events.title: North and south queues begin building
events.description: Traffic waiting outside the canal turned the blockage into a visibly systemic disruption, with every delayed ship representing wider schedule and cargo effects.

events.title: Dredging and tug operations intensify
events.description: The response shifted into a race between salvage progress and the economic pressure created by waiting traffic and early rerouting decisions.

events.title: Ever Given is refloated
events.description: The refloating ended the physical blockage but not the operational problem. Queued ships still had to be sequenced through the canal.

events.title: Backlog clearance begins
events.description: Convoy sequencing and catch-up operations became the next phase of the story as operators tried to understand how much of the network shock would persist downstream.

events.title: Ship released after compensation dispute
events.description: The incident moved from salvage into liability, compensation, and reputational questions about who bore the cost of the disruption.

meta.name: Grounding site
meta.description: The location where the ship obstructed the full channel width and shut traffic in both directions.

meta.name: Great Bitter Lake
meta.description: Key internal holding area used during post-refloating traffic management and sequencing.

meta.name: Port Said queue
meta.description: Anchor zone for ships waiting on the Mediterranean side of the canal.

meta.name: Suez anchorage queue
meta.description: Anchor zone for ships waiting on the Red Sea side of the canal.

meta.name: Cape of Good Hope detour
meta.description: A simplified diversion line showing the long alternate path that some ships considered or took while the canal remained blocked.
